title: Stereo Depth Estimation
concept: Stereo depth estimation uses two cameras positioned apart, measuring how much a point shifts between the two views (disparity) to triangulate its distance — mimicking how human binocular vision perceives depth.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can't 2D image classification alone provide the distance information needed for drone collision avoidance?

options.text: Classification only identifies object categories, not per-pixel distance from the camera
Classification always includes exact distance measurements by default
Drones cannot use cameras at all for navigation

exercises.explain: Yes! Classification identifies categories, not distances.

cards.title: Close one eye and try to judge distance
cards.body: Try closing one eye and quickly reaching to touch a nearby object — it's noticeably harder to judge exact distance with just one eye. Your brain normally uses the SLIGHT difference between what your two eyes see to triangulate depth.

cards.title: Stereo Depth Estimation
cards.body: Stereo depth estimation uses two cameras placed a known distance apart (like human eyes). By measuring the disparity — how much a point's position shifts between the left and right camera images — the system triangulates that point's actual distance using geometry.

cards.title: The classic finger parallax trick
cards.body: Hold your finger close to your face and alternately close each eye — your finger appears to 'jump' left and right dramatically. Now hold it far away and do the same — it barely moves. That jump amount (disparity) directly reveals distance: big jump means close, small jump means far.

cards.title: Self-driving car stereo rigs
cards.body: Some self-driving car systems mount two cameras a fixed distance apart on the vehicle. By matching the same road features (like a stop sign) between the left and right images and measuring their pixel-position disparity, the system computes exactly how far that stop sign is from the car.

cards.title: Stereo needs matching features to work
cards.body: It's a myth that stereo depth estimation works on any surface effortlessly. A perfectly blank, textureless wall (like plain white paint) gives the matching algorithm nothing distinctive to match between the two camera views, making disparity — and therefore depth — hard to estimate accurately in those regions.

cards.title: Disparity and Distance
cards.body: How pixel shift relates to real-world distance:

left.text:
Large pixel shift between views
Large disparity
Computed as CLOSE distance

right.text:
Small pixel shift between views
Small disparity
Computed as FAR distance

exercises.prompt: What is 'disparity' in stereo depth estimation?

options.text: How much a point's position shifts between the left and right camera images
The exact color difference between two cameras' images
The total number of pixels in an image
The frame rate difference between two video streams

exercises.explain: Correct! Disparity is the positional shift used to triangulate distance.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A larger disparity between the two stereo views indicates the point is FARTHER from the cameras.
exercises.explain: False. A LARGER disparity indicates the point is CLOSER; smaller disparity indicates farther distance.

exercises.sentence: Stereo depth estimation triangulates distance by measuring how much a point shifts, called ___, between two camera views.
exercises.explain: Disparity is the key measurement used to triangulate depth in stereo vision.

exercises.prompt: Why might stereo depth estimation struggle on a completely blank, textureless white wall?

options.text: Without distinctive features, the matching algorithm has nothing reliable to match between the left and right camera views
White walls physically block both cameras from capturing any image
Textureless surfaces always produce perfectly accurate disparity by default

exercises.explain: Correct! Feature matching requires distinctive texture, which blank surfaces lack.

exercises.prompt: An engineer notices their stereo depth system produces very unreliable depth estimates specifically in a large, empty, plain-colored warehouse aisle, but works fine on shelves full of varied products. What is the likely cause?

options.text: The plain, textureless aisle surfaces give the stereo matching algorithm few distinctive features to match between the two camera views, making disparity estimation unreliable there
Stereo cameras physically cannot function in warehouses under any circumstances
The shelves are actively jamming the camera's ability to see the aisle
This behavior is completely unrelated to surface texture

exercises.explain: Exactly! Textureless regions are a known weak point for stereo matching. Next: estimating depth from just ONE camera — monocular depth estimation.
